key_points: ASA Criterion: Constructing a triangle given two angles and the side between them
RHS Criterion: Constructing a right-angled triangle with the hypotenuse and one side
Semi-regular tilings use two or more types of regular polygons
AAS (Angle-Angle-Side) can be converted to ASA using the Angle Sum Property ($180^{\circ}$)
examples: ASA: Base 6 cm, angles $30^{\circ}$ and $70^{\circ}$ at the ends
RHS: Right angle at B, Hypotenuse AC = 10 cm, Side AB = 6 cm
Semi-regular Tiling: A pattern of octagons and squares meeting at a vertex
AAS Conversion: If $\angle A=40^{\circ}, \angle B=60^{\circ}$, then $\angle C = 180^{\circ} - (40^{\circ}+60^{\circ}) = 80^{\circ}$
formulas: Angle Sum Property $ightarrow \angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180^{\circ}$
RHS condition $ightarrow 	ext{Side}^2 + 	ext{Base}^2 = 	ext{Hypotenuse}^2$ (Pythagoras link)
exam_tips: In RHS construction, the 'swing' of the compass for the hypotenuse must start from the non-right-angle vertex
For ASA, if the given side is not between the angles, calculate the third angle first
When justifying a tiling, show that the angles meeting at a vertex add up to exactly $360^{\circ}$
Don't erase your construction arcs; they are proof of your method
